title: Women of the Reformation: Louise de Coligny
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: Women of the Reformation_ Louise de Coligny - Elder Nick Anderson.txt
outline: Introduction: The regular study of historical characters in Reformation history
Life and background of the subject
Her encounter with the Reformed faith
Her influence and contributions to the Reformation
Challenges she faced as a woman of faith
Her lasting legacy for the church
Conclusion: Learning from Women of the Reformation
summary: Elder Nick Anderson presents a study of Louise de Coligny, daughter of the Huguenot admiral Gaspard de Coligny and later Princess of Orange. He explores her remarkable life and faith through the French Wars of Religion and her role in the Protestant cause.
transcript:
## Review: Marguerite de Navarre

Before we turn to Louise de Coligny, let us briefly review what we learned last week about Marguerite de Navarre. She influenced Queen Elizabeth I through her own daughter and through her relationship with Anne Boleyn. She was a queen, well-educated, and versed in multiple languages. Even secular historians who were not friendly to Christianity could not help praising her genuine faith. She never formally left the Roman Catholic Church but was the protector and champion of the Huguenots throughout her life. She was a poet, a playwright, a writer — a true Renaissance woman. She became the primary Christian influence sheltering the Protestant movement in France during one of its most dangerous periods, and she was close friends with John Calvin. That was last week.

## Louise de Coligny: Background and Family

This week we will be looking at Louise de Coligny, born September 23, 1555, the daughter of Gaspard de Coligny and Charlotte de Laval. We know virtually nothing about her childhood until she was about fifteen or sixteen, but we know a great deal about her parents and what kind of people they were.

Gaspard de Coligny was a French nobleman who spent his youth in sports and military exercises in preparation for military service. He eventually became an admiral in the navy and, more importantly, a leader of the Huguenots. He was raised Catholic, as most people in France were. At some point — the exact circumstances are a bit mysterious — he and the whole family became staunch Huguenots. The most common account is that he was converted while reading Calvin's *Institutes* during his imprisonment after the Battle of St. Quentin. Another biographer says he was simply reading the Scriptures. Either way, Calvin himself wrote to him and said that God had brought this affliction upon him "in order that he might be withdrawn from the distractions of the world and be able to give his undivided attention to God's voice speaking through the Scriptures" — and indeed, that is what happened.

Upon his release from prison in 1559, Gaspard returned to France and publicly professed his adherence to the reformed faith. Before taking this public stance, he spoke to his wife Charlotte and said: "Are you willing to profess the reformed faith with me?" He reminded her that this was risky — people who were reformed had been burned at the stake, their possessions confiscated. Her reply tells you what kind of woman she was: "This has always been the case in the Church of Christ" — and she pledged her reformed faith. They were devout Huguenots, and their hope was that their daughters would remain faithful to Christ, carry on that legacy, defend the faith, and advance it. They took great risks for their faith, and they paid a heavy price.

As admiral and Huguenot leader, Gaspard was a very busy man, but he wrote regularly to his daughters to encourage them — specifically, that they should not mourn the loss of worldly things, for we have treasure in heaven, and no one can take that away. This would profoundly influence Louise throughout her life.

## First Marriage and the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre

Louise was married at about fifteen to Charles de Téligny, a Protestant leader who served alongside her father — about twenty years her senior. There was a brief period of peace between Catholics and Protestants, and they took that opportunity to marry. The peace did not last long.

A little historical background: In 1570, the Treaty of St. Germain was signed as a new attempt to reconcile Catholics and Protestants in France, ending the latest religious war. However, the treaty angered the hardcore Catholics, particularly the Guise family, who thought it was too favorable to the Protestants. Gaspard, who had become close with the king, urged France to enter a war in the Netherlands alongside the Prince of Orange against Philip II of Spain, hoping that Catholics and Protestants fighting side by side might bring reconciliation. It did not work out that way.

Soon after this suggestion, an assassination attempt was made on Gaspard's life. He was shot and wounded while leaving the Louvre but did not die immediately. Tensions in Paris were already very high. Just one day later — August 23, 1572 — a council was held at which it was decided that Gaspard must be killed and other Huguenot leaders eliminated as well.

The following day was August 24, 1572. Louise and Charles were asleep when she woke to the sound of bells ringing. She lay in the darkness, confused, hearing muffled voices and commotion downstairs. The voices grew louder; she could hear shouts echoing up the stairs. She sat up and whispered to her husband: "What is going on? Why are the bells ringing?" Charles got up, went to the door, and opened it just enough to listen. They heard a voice call for a minister named Merlin. They heard her father's voice. They heard Gaspard ask: "Merlin, will you pray with me?"

Louise got out of bed and went toward the door. Charles was holding her back. They heard Gaspard and Merlin praying together. Then Charles grabbed her and told her to stay there. Other voices rang through the halls. They heard Gaspard say, "My Lord calls me to himself." They heard him call back to the man at his side: "I am prepared to die. I need no more help of men. Save yourselves, my friends, if it is still possible."

Charles pushed Louise back into the room, shut the door, and left — presumably to go for help, or to go to the Louvre. So Louise was now alone in the dark. She did not know what was happening. She only knew it was not good, and there was nothing she could do. She heard shouts, more commotion, and eventually gunshots.

Charles made his way to the Louvre, but in a hallway he was confronted by armed men who demanded he recant his faith. He refused, and was killed immediately — one of the first victims of the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre. Gaspard, who had told everyone to save themselves, was abandoned by all except one man, Nicholas Muss, who stayed and was murdered alongside him. Gaspard's body was thrown from the window into the street below as a symbol of Catholic triumph. The crowd cut off his head and hands. That was the beginning of the massacre.

Somehow — we do not know how — Louise managed to escape. She was probably on foot, probably alone. We know that the Catholics killed all the Protestants they found: men, women, and children. All but two of the gates of Paris had been closed. Somehow she found one that was open and made her way out. She went immediately to her father's castle in Châtillon in Burgundy to warn her family. She arrived so quickly that they had not yet heard anything about the massacre. They fled with the clothes on their backs. They decided to split up. Louise and a cousin fled together. Her brothers managed to escape. Her mother was able to flee. Her youngest brother was captured and returned to Paris. Louise made it to Geneva, then to Bern, and finally to Basel.

In one night, Louise became a widow, an exile, an orphan, and impoverished. The St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre would continue for three days in Paris alone, with as many as four thousand Protestants killed simply for being Protestants. As news spread, violence broke out in surrounding provinces, and from August to September, at least ten thousand more Protestants were killed across France. As we discussed last week, France effectively destroyed its own Protestant faith in those days and has never fully recovered.

## The Next Ten Years and Remarriage

The next ten years of Louise's life are largely unknown. We know that at one point she returned to Paris to try to recover some of her father's possessions. Then in 1583, William of Orange in the Netherlands proposed marriage to her. She accepted. He was content to marry a poor woman with no dowry; she was happy to marry a man of admirable character and goals. They married in Antwerp and settled in Delft. Though the Dutch people were initially suspicious of a French woman marrying their leader, they would come to love her.

She was described as "a small, well-formed woman with delicate features, exquisite complexion and very beautiful dark eyes which seemed in after years to be dim with unshed tears, with remarkable powers of mind and jealous sweetness of disposition." William's daughter wrote that "the new mother loves us all and takes very good care of us." Louise and William had a son together, Prince Frederick Henry, who would later take his father's title and position.

## The Death of William and Its Aftermath

Louise was fearful that William would also be murdered — and this was not irrational. There had already been an assassination attempt on his life in 1582. Philip II of Spain had placed a large reward on William's head.

That threat would be realized. A man named Gérard — a Frenchman, a staunch Catholic from Spanish-controlled territory — had heard of the price on William's head. He had been planning the murder for years. He came to Delft in 1584 under a false name, took a position in William's service, and carried a Bible to feign Protestant sympathies. He appeared before William one day with some need, and William generously gave him money to buy shoes.

One evening at dinner, Louise was looking around the room and her eye fell on Gérard. Something about his demeanor made her suspicious. She leaned over to her husband and said: "Who is that sinister-looking man?" William glanced at Gérard, gave him a nod, and said to his wife: "That man wants a passport, and I intend to give it to him."

Gérard left the room. Shortly after, William followed, going to a hallway at the foot of the stairs. Gérard was standing there. He had papers in one hand and a pistol concealed in the other. As William glanced away, Gérard drew the pistol and shot him three times at point-blank range. William cried out, "Oh my God, have mercy on my soul, and upon this poor people!" He was carried to his bed and died on July 10, 1584.

Louise was thirty-two years old. She was now twice widowed. Under her care were a handful of stepchildren, an eighteen-year-old son Maurice who was suddenly the leader of the Netherlands at war with Spain, her own six-month-old son, and no income at all.

One biographer wrote of her: "She had the advantage to be sprung from the greatest man in Europe and to have had two husbands of very eminent virtue, the last of whom left behind him an immortal reputation. But she likewise had the misfortune to lose them all three by hasty and violent deaths... Her life had been nothing but a continuous series of afflictions, able to make any soul sink under them — but a soul like hers had resigned itself entirely to the will of God."

Louise had been married to William for only fifteen months. She wrote to his brother that she hardly knew how she was going to provide for her family. But the Dutch provinces, who loved her, gave her a yearly allowance of twenty thousand francs that would sustain her for the rest of her life.

## Service and Influence in the Netherlands

With all these tragic events around her, Louise did not crumble. She set herself to advising and influencing wherever she could. Maurice was only eighteen and needed all the help he could get. Louise established international connections with the British, and the alliance she helped forge would bring British troops to the aid of the Dutch against the Spanish army. She wrote to Queen Elizabeth I asking for help. Elizabeth sent troops to the Netherlands, and they were able to push back the Spanish forces that had occupied Dutch cities.

Louise continued to be active in foreign affairs, advising Maurice on political matters, and maintaining correspondence with British and French leaders. The education of William's daughters was in her charge; she faithfully instructed five of them, and it was said that some of those children resembled her character more than that of their own natural mothers.

France was never far from her mind. When Henry of Navarre became King of France and eventually made it safe — and then legal — for Huguenots to live there, Louise returned to France in 1598. She moved back to the Netherlands in 1603 when religious controversies broke out between Calvinists and Arminians. She moved to The Hague and helped found a French Reformed church there.

However, her pastor in that church became something of a leader on the Arminian side of the controversy. Louise sided with her pastor — perhaps out of loyalty to the man who had helped found the church. When the Arminian ministers were expelled by the Dutch government, she returned to France in 1620.

## Her Death and Legacy

In 1620, Louise became seriously ill. Word spread quickly, and Roman Catholic leaders saw their opportunity. Cardinal Richelieu — known for being a hard and manipulative man — was sent to her side. He entered the room and saw two Protestants there with her: a Reformed pastor and a Protestant friend. His opening words were: "Madam, take care of your soul. You have two evil spirits beside you." He then expressed his deep anxiety for her soul and urged her to return to the Catholic faith before she died.

But her faith was strong and she did not relent. Richelieu was unsuccessful. She held firm to her faith in Christ. Shortly before her death, despite having sided with her Arminian pastor in that earlier controversy, she professed that she was unmoved in her reformed beliefs. In the face of Cardinal Richelieu she said: **"I rely solely on Christ's merits for my salvation."**

She died on October 9, 1620. In her will she provided for many servants and many believers. Reformed Protestants of all stripes mourned her death.

## Four Lessons from the Life of Louise de Coligny

**First: Do not love this world.** This is what her father urged her toward in those letters he wrote when she was young — do not mourn the loss of worldly things if we have treasure in heaven, because no one can take that away. What are the treasures that we hold onto? Are they the things in heaven that cannot be taken away, or are they our cars, our reputation, our possessions, our health, our families? Think: if your own family was taken from you suddenly, as Louise's was, would you still trust Christ? Would you say with Job, "Blessed be the name of the Lord"? We ought to search our hearts.

> *"Do not love the world or the things in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him."* (1 John 2:15)

Calvin comments on this passage: "This vanity — that is, the love of this world — must first be torn away from us if we are to love God." Louise could have recanted. She had many opportunities to do so. She could have returned to France as a comfortable Roman Catholic. She did not treasure this world more than Christ.

**Second: Be unmovable in your faith.** The first requirement of being unmovable is knowing the Scriptures and knowing the God of the Scriptures. A recent survey found that 43% of self-described evangelicals agreed with the statement "Jesus was a great teacher, but he was not God." If that statistic is accurate, the evangelical church does not know the Scriptures. We need to know the Word better than we know our favorite television show, our favorite book series, our favorite sports team. The God who sustained Louise is your God. The God of Moses, who led Israel through the Red Sea on dry ground. The God of David, who defeated Goliath. The God of Daniel, who shut the mouth of the lion. Read Hebrews 11. That is your God.

**Third: Resign yourself completely to the will of God.** Esther said, "If I perish, I perish." Christ prayed in Gethsemane, "Not as I will, but as you will." Can we truly say that? Louise's testimony, wherever she found herself — advising a young prince on political matters, educating her stepchildren, defying a cardinal on her deathbed — is of a woman content to do whatever God had for her to do in that moment.

**Fourth: Use your suffering well.** Do not let suffering distract or derail your obedience. Be thankful for suffering. Learn from it. Meditate on Christ's suffering for you. Most of us suffer very little. We watch the news, see that gas prices are rising, and feel consumed with anxiety. Louise's family was being murdered in the streets, and she remained faithful to Christ. We get a hang nail and we want to write a lament. We need to learn from those who have gone before us what it actually looks like to suffer well — and to suffer for Christ.
